This enables aggregation of the reductions (in NCCL 2.1 it is not a real aggregation, since each reduction is being done in its own launch and they benefit from lack of synchronization between reductions in a group, but NCCL 2.2 will introduce real aggregation support). 
It needs to be done in the frontend, because kvstore itself does not have any information on which gradients should be aggregated, in which order and how much time is it supposed to wait before real launch - mxnet's dependency engine does not really allow for that. That's why we need to provide data about all of the reductions in a group to benefit from aggregation.

I feel like we're doing some rather ugly and convoluted stuff to work around a lack of `read_string` in some versions of ConfigParser; what if we monkeypatched it in before doing any of this:

Up near the top of the file, right after the imports, we can do the following:

```python
if not hasattr(ConfigParser, 'read_string'):
    # work around earlier versions of ConfigParser that do not support `read_string`
    def cp_read_string(self, string, source='<string>'):
        self.readfp(six.StringIO(string), filename=source)
    ConfigParser.read_string = cp_read_string
```

Then we can remove all of this logic from `AirflowConfigParser` and use `self.read_string` or `self.defaults.read_string`.

I think it is more canonical to have generators consume the items as they iterate. The advantage of having the storage consume the items is that you won't have to call `clear` at the end.

That of course would be O(N) for the default thread local attrs, since its a list (array). But if we change the thread local attrs to use a `deque`, we could do this in constant time (we'd want to make sure to communicate this access pattern in the docstrings so that the implementors of span storage can be sure to use a deque/linked-list).

Another alternative would be to have a method to return an iterator over the items and to not have the storage itself be iterable.

The `ignore` patterns make this a bit challenging I think... the patterns used here are relative to the root of the repository/buildroot, and so we construct the `Gitignore` instance with an empty root. But if you were going to be capturing from a subdirectory of the repository and you still wanted to apply the same patterns, you'd need to pass in the portion of the subdirectory relative to the buildroot (afaik).

This is to account for patterns like:
```
$ cat .gitignore
/please/ignore/this/long/specific/file.sh
```

I think that not applying ignore patterns to this type of capture would be reasonable, and would probably be fine for now. But if we're going to apply them, I think we'd need to re-construct them as relative to the buildroot (if the capture was under the buildroot), or ignore the patterns if the capture was outside the buildroot.

>What was wrong with the extras as the were? They had a clean isolation of which dependencies to install for which tests.

I haven't had a good mental model of the dependencies before @pipermerriam explained it further. I'm still probably lacking some important details, but here comes my second take nevertheless.

Let's start with this: I changed the `install_requires` to 

```
install_requires = deps['evm'] + deps['p2p'] + deps['trinity']
```

Because I believe anyone who runs `pip install py-evm` does expect this to install `evm`, `p2p` and `trinity` fully to the point that no further manual installs are needed. At least for now that `p2p` and `trinity` are part of this package.

That's why extras such as `p2p` or `trinity` are gone because they are simply installed as part of the regular dependencies.

However, based on the feedback that I received I separated out a new section `evm-extras`

```
    'evm-extras': [
        "coincurve>=7.0.0,<8.0.0",
        "plyvel==1.0.4",
        "eth-hash[pycryptodome]",
    ]
``` 
Previously these were separate sections (`coincurve` and `leveldb`) and  `"eth-hash[pycryptodome]"` was living under `requirements-dev.txt` (which is now gone). It made sense to me to group these under one section but maybe there are good reasons to have them separately? I think it's more convenient to have them grouped and yet people can still install separately whatever they choose.

>In the ideal setup, we shouldn't need to install the "developer" extra inside the test suite at all

Yes, I agree and in fact, the tests now only need `evm-extras` and `test` as extras whereas the linting jobs just need `lint` as extras.

Post migration name : 

Standard to premium migration feature does not migrate the data from the Standard Namespace to the Premium namespace. Given this, The recommended workflow to migrate an application from Standard to Premium, without incurring delays in message processing is as follows

1.	Start-Migration. Which starts replicating the nested resources from Standard namespace to Premium Namespace  AND creates the DNS record with the Post Migration name.
2.	User creates a listener application with the Post Migration namespace name, to drain the messages post migration.
3.	Complete-Migration. Which will repoint the standard namespace DNS to the Premium namespace at which point all the Send and Receive operations to the standard namespace are routed to the Premium namespace.

To enable this workflow, the post-migration DNS is created as part of Start-Migration.

This works. But you'll get more useful assertion error messages if you first check that the correct keys are present before checking the value of those keys.

Imagine for a moment that this assertion fails with a `KeyError`, and exception states that the no "display_name" attribute exists. In this case, you know that the server didn't return a "display_name" key, but you're left wondering what other attributes the server returned. Did the server return a "description" key? An assertion like this tells you for sure:

python
self.assertLessEqual(
    {'id', 'display_name', 'description'},
    keys(self.repo.last_response.json())
)
```

You can then go ahead and check the value of each key.

Nope, sorry! Take `get` as an example:

1. Thread 1 executes `if id not in _global_cache`, sees that there's nothing in the cache, but is then interrupted.
2. Thread 2 jumps in, also sees nothing in the cache, allocates a new instance, o2, adds it to the cache, and returns o2 to the caller.
3. Thread 1 wakes up again and continues by allocating a new instance, o1, adding it to the cache, and returning o1 to the caller.

You now have one client with o1, one with o2, and a cache containing o1 (although it could just as easily be o2 in the cache, with o1 as the "floating" instance). Next time a client calls in, it will get the cached version but the service may be inconsistent by then.

Note: there are other points where an interrupt could occur, with similar effects.

But, like I said, maybe it doesn't actually matter for the current use case.

@guoyuhong When importing multiple items from a module. I prefer to put one item per line:
```
from x.y.z import (
  a,
  b,
  c,
)
```

The reason is because:
1) the item names are naturally sorted.
2) if we add a new item in the future, we only add a new line, without touching  the existing lines. It makes it easy to `git blame` which commit introduced the item. (Note there's a comma in the last line as well.)

```python
from tensorpack import *
import tensorflow as tf

enable_argscope_for_module(tf.layers)

def build(x):
    return tf.layers.conv2d(x, 32, [3, 3], name='conv0')
ans = []

print "----------------------------------"
plhdr = tf.placeholder(tf.float32, [1, 28, 28, 3])
ans.append(build(plhdr))

print "----------------------------------"
with TowerContext('tower0', True, vs_name='tower0') as ctx:
    ans.append(build(plhdr))

print "----------------------------------"
with TowerContext('tower1', True, vs_name='tower1') as ctx:
    ans.append(build(plhdr))

print "----------------------------------"
with TowerContext('tower2', True, vs_name='tower2') as ctx:
    ans.append(build(plhdr))


```
